summary: O'Neil acknowledges her prediction of 600 daily COVID deaths in Florida was wrong—the actual figure is 184—but argues the discrepancy may be due to data manipulation rather than genuine success. She points to narrow definitions of COVID deaths, lagging reporting, and changing methodologies as reasons to doubt Florida's official numbers. The post links to her Bloomberg column exploring why Florida's death toll appears lower than New York's despite high case counts.
key_points: Her prediction of 600 daily deaths was off by a factor of three, with actual numbers at 184
Florida's data practices—narrow death definitions, reporting lags, methodology changes—raise credibility concerns
The apparent success in keeping deaths low may be an artifact of how deaths are counted rather than actual outcomes
She maintains epistemic humility while still questioning official narratives
money_quotes: I was wrong to predict 600 daily deaths by COVID in Florida by now; right now it's at 184.
But the fishy data coming out of Florida makes me think I'm not as wrong as I wish I were.
Narrow definitions of what counts as a COVID death, lagging data, and changing methodologies make me doubt the official numbers.
key_insight: Being wrong about a prediction doesn't mean the underlying analysis was flawed when the data itself is unreliable.
